verdict_reasoning: ITPR3 (inositol 1,4,5-trisphosphate receptor 3) is a ligand-gated intracellular calcium channel anchored to the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) membrane. It is a multi-pass transmembrane protein but its topology and function are exclusively ER-resident. The protein is embedded in the ER membrane with its pore facing the ER lumen; it is not trafficked to the plasma membrane under physiological conditions. No documented surface-display isoform, ectodomain-shedding, or regulated exocytosis brings the ITPR3 protein body to the outer leaflet of the PM. Although intracellular calcium signaling is therapeutic target-relevant, ITPR3 itself is not accessible to extracellular binders. Ruling out contextual buckets: (1) cell-state induction — no evidence ITPR3 surfaces under stress, transformation, or infection; (2) tissue restriction — ITPR3 is ubiquitously expressed; (3) lysosomal exocytosis — ITPR3 is ER-resident, not late-endosomal; (4) dual localization — no PM pool documented; (5) stable surface attachment — no secreted ectodomain or PM-anchored isoform.
